questions.q: State Kirchhoff's current law and the conservation principle it expresses.
options: The voltages around a loop sum to zero; conservation of charge
The currents into a junction equal the currents out; conservation of charge
The currents into a junction equal the currents out; conservation of energy
The resistances in a loop sum to zero; conservation of energy
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's current law states that the total current into a junction equals the total current out (ΣI = 0), expressing conservation of charge — charge can't pile up or vanish at a junction. (The voltage law, ΣV = 0 around a loop, expresses conservation of energy.) Naming the conservation principle is the mark-earning move.

questions.q: State Kirchhoff's voltage law and the conservation principle it expresses.
options: The currents at a junction sum to zero; conservation of energy
The voltages around any closed loop sum to zero; conservation of energy
The voltages around a loop sum to zero; conservation of charge
The supply voltage equals the largest resistor's voltage; conservation of charge
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's voltage law states that around any closed loop the voltages sum to zero — the voltage supplied equals the total dropped across the components — expressing conservation of energy (a charge gains as much energy from the source as it loses round the loop). The current law expresses conservation of charge.

questions.q: Identify which quantity is the same through every component in a series circuit.
questions.explanation: In a series circuit there is a single path, so the current is the same through every component, while the voltage divides among them in proportion to resistance. In a parallel circuit it is the voltage that is the same across each branch, while the current divides.

questions.q: Compare how the total resistance changes when resistors are added in series versus in parallel.
options: Series adds (total increases); parallel uses the reciprocal sum (total decreases, below the smallest branch)
questions.explanation: In series, resistances add (R_T = R₁ + R₂ + …), so adding components increases the total. In parallel, 1/R_T = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + …, so adding branches decreases the total, which is always less than the smallest branch — extra paths ease the current flow. Thinking 'more resistors = more resistance' (true only in series) is the trap.

questions.q: Three resistors of 2 Ω, 3 Ω and 5 Ω are connected in series with a 20 V supply. Calculate the current.
questions.explanation: In series the resistances add: R_T = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 Ω. Then I = V/R_T = 20/10 = 2 A, the same current through all three. Treating the circuit as parallel, or not summing the resistances, is the common error.

questions.q: A 6 Ω and a 3 Ω resistor are connected in parallel across 12 V. Calculate the total resistance.
questions.explanation: 1/R_T = 1/6 + 1/3 = 1/6 + 2/6 = 3/6 = 1/2, so R_T = 2 Ω — less than the smallest branch (3 Ω), as expected for parallel. Forgetting to take the reciprocal at the end (leaving ½ Ω) or adding the resistances (9 Ω) are the common errors.

questions.q: In the 6 Ω ∥ 3 Ω parallel combination across 12 V, calculate the current through the 3 Ω branch.
questions.explanation: Each branch has the full 12 V across it, so I = V/R = 12/3 = 4 A through the 3 Ω branch (and 12/6 = 2 A through the 6 Ω branch). The lower-resistance branch carries the larger current, and the branch currents (2 + 4 = 6 A) add to the total — Kirchhoff's current law.

questions.q: Explain why connecting resistors in parallel decreases the total resistance.
options: Because it provides extra alternative paths, allowing more total current for the same voltage, which means lower resistance
Because the voltage across each branch increases
questions.explanation: Parallel branches provide additional paths between the same two points, so more total current can flow for the same supply voltage. By Ohm's law, a larger current for the same voltage means a smaller overall resistance — and the total is always less than the smallest branch.

questions.q: Identify why household circuits are wired in parallel rather than in series.
options: So one appliance failing cuts power to all the others
So each appliance gets the full supply voltage and can be switched independently
To increase the total resistance of the house
So the same current flows through every appliance
questions.explanation: In parallel, every appliance gets the full supply voltage and has its own path, so each can be switched on/off independently and one failing doesn't break the others. (In series, one break would stop everything.) The branch currents add, which is why fuses/breakers cap the total.

questions.q: A 12 V supply connects a 3 Ω resistor in series with a parallel pair of 6 Ω resistors. Calculate the total resistance.
questions.explanation: First reduce the parallel pair: 1/R = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3, so 3 Ω. This is in series with the 3 Ω resistor: R_T = 3 + 3 = 6 Ω. The method is always reduce the parallel section first, then add the series part. Adding all three as if in series (15 Ω) is the trap.
